task: I’ve got a routine procedure coming up, and I want to be sure I’m not forgetting anything basic before and after. Could you help me make a simple checklist of what to ask, what to have ready at home, and what signs would mean I should call the doctor rather than just wait it out?

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a simple, easy-to-use checklist you can bring with you.

## Before the procedure: what to ask
- What exactly will be done?
- Will I need to fast, stop any medicines, or avoid anything before it?
- What should I bring the day of the procedure?
- How long will it take?
- Will I need someone to drive me home?
- What should I expect afterward: pain, tiredness, swelling, bleeding, etc.?
- What is normal recovery time?
- What symptoms mean I should call the office?
- When should I follow up?

## Have ready at home
- Your discharge instructions / paperwork
- A list of your medicines and doses
- Any prescribed pain medicine or other meds you may need
- A thermometer, if you have one
- Phone numbers for the doctor’s office and after-hours line
- Comfortable clothes, snacks, and water
- A ride home if needed
- A quiet place to rest
- Any supplies they tell you to use, like bandages or ice packs

## Call the doctor if you notice
- Fever or chills
- Bleeding that won’t stop or gets worse
- Severe pain that isn’t improving
- Swelling that is getting worse
- Redness, warmth, pus, or bad-smelling drainage
- Trouble breathing
- Chest pain
- Dizziness, fainting, or confusion
- Vomiting that won’t stop
- You can’t keep fluids down
- Anything that feels suddenly worse instead of gradually better

## Good to mention before you leave
- Any allergies or past bad reactions
- Blood thinners or other medicines you take
- Any chance you could be pregnant, if that applies
- Any trouble with anesthesia or sedation in the past
- Any concerns about pain control, walking, eating, or doing things at home

If you want, I can turn this into a very short one-page checklist you can copy into your notes app or print.

user_simulator.text:
Yes, that’s the sort of plain checklist I wanted. Clear enough to use, not fussy. I’d probably print it and keep it with my papers.

One thing I’d add: ask them straight out whether you can shower, drive, or take your regular medicine the day of and the day after. That’s the part people forget, and it matters more than it sounds.
